_meta.description: Certification-level assessment questions for Cohort agents. Sources: PCAP (Python Institute), JSE (JS Institute), ISTQB CTFL v4.0, OWASP Top 10 2025, IAAP WAS (Web Accessibility Specialist).

questions.question:
What is the output of the following code?

def func(a, b=[]):
    b.append(a)
    return b

print(func(1))
print(func(2))
questions.explanation: Mutable default arguments persist between calls. The list b is created once at definition time.

questions.question: Which statement about Python's GIL (Global Interpreter Lock) is TRUE?

choices.A: It prevents all forms of concurrent execution in Python
choices.B: It allows only one thread to execute Python bytecode at a time in CPython
choices.C: It is present in all Python implementations including PyPy and Jython
choices.D: It makes Python programs thread-safe by default

questions.explanation: The GIL is specific to CPython and allows only one thread to execute bytecode at a time, though I/O-bound threads can release it.

choices.B: B
choices.C: A then B

questions.explanation: ZeroDivisionError is a subclass of ArithmeticError. The first matching except block executes, so 'A' prints.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct way to open a file for reading in Python while ensuring it is properly closed?

choices.B: with open('file.txt', 'r') as f: data = f.read()
choices.C: f = open('file.txt'); data = f.read()  # Python auto-closes
choices.D: Both A and B are correct and equally safe

questions.explanation: The with statement (context manager) guarantees the file is closed even if an exception occurs. Option A can leak the handle on exception.

questions.question: What is the output of: list(map(lambda x: x**2, filter(lambda x: x % 2 == 0, range(6))))?
questions.explanation: filter keeps even numbers from range(6): [0, 2, 4]. map squares them: [0, 4, 16].

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the difference between @staticmethod and @classmethod?

choices.A: @staticmethod receives the class as first argument; @classmethod does not
choices.B: @classmethod receives the class (cls) as first argument; @staticmethod receives neither self nor cls
choices.C: Both receive self as first argument but differ in inheritance behavior
choices.D: There is no practical difference; they are interchangeable

questions.explanation: @classmethod gets cls (the class) as first parameter. @staticmethod gets no implicit first parameter -- it's just a regular function namespaced in the class.

questions.question: What happens when you execute: import this?

choices.A: ImportError -- 'this' is not a standard module
choices.B: It prints The Zen of Python
choices.C: It returns a reference to the current module

questions.explanation: 'import this' is an Easter egg that prints The Zen of Python (PEP 20), Python's design principles.
Dict comprehension swaps keys and values. v becomes the new key, k becomes the new value.

questions.question:
What is the output of:

a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a
b.append(4)
print(a)

choices.C: TypeError

questions.explanation: b = a creates a reference, not a copy. Both a and b point to the same list object. Modifying b modifies a.

questions.question: Which approach is correct for making a SQL query safe from injection in Python?

choices.B: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + user_id)
choices.C: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = %s", (user_id,))
choices.D: cursor.execute("SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = " + str(int(user_id)))

questions.explanation: Parameterized queries (%s with tuple) let the database driver handle escaping. String formatting (A, B) and manual casting (D) are injection-prone.

questions.question:
What is the output of:
console.log(typeof null);

choices.B: "undefined"
choices.C: "object"

questions.explanation: typeof null returns 'object' -- this is a well-known JavaScript bug from the language's first implementation that was never fixed for backwards compatibility.

choices.C: TypeError -- cannot modify const

questions.explanation: const prevents reassignment of the variable, not mutation of the object. The array itself can be modified with push, pop, etc.

questions.question:
What is the output of:
console.log(0.1 + 0.2 === 0.3);

choices.B: false
choices.C: NaN

questions.explanation: IEEE 754 floating-point arithmetic: 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004, which is not strictly equal to 0.3.

questions.question: In React, what happens when you call setState with the same value as the current state?

choices.B: React bails out of the re-render (shallow comparison)
choices.C: React throws a warning in development mode
choices.D: React queues the update but delays rendering indefinitely

questions.explanation: React uses Object.is() for comparison. If the new state is the same as current state, React skips re-rendering (bail out optimization).

questions.question: What is the key difference between Promise.all() and Promise.allSettled()?

choices.A: Promise.all() runs sequentially; Promise.allSettled() runs in parallel
choices.B: Promise.all() rejects on first failure; Promise.allSettled() waits for all promises to settle
choices.C: Promise.allSettled() is synchronous; Promise.all() is asynchronous
choices.D: There is no difference; allSettled is just an alias

questions.explanation: Promise.all() short-circuits on first rejection. Promise.allSettled() always waits for every promise to either resolve or reject, returning status for each.

choices.B: null
choices.C: undefined

questions.explanation: The ? makes age optional. When not provided, accessing it returns undefined (not null or 0).

questions.question: Which hook should you use to perform side effects (data fetching, subscriptions) in a React functional component?

choices.B: useCallback
choices.C: useEffect

questions.explanation: useEffect is the hook for side effects. useMemo/useCallback are for memoization. useRef is for mutable references that don't trigger re-renders.
Spread operator creates a shallow copy. Modifying b doesn't affect a. a remains [1,2,3] (length 3), b becomes [1,2,3,4] (length 4).

questions.question: What does 'use strict' at the top of a JavaScript file do?

choices.A: Enables TypeScript-like type checking at runtime
choices.B: Prevents assigning to undeclared variables and makes silent errors throw exceptions
choices.C: Activates async/await syntax support

questions.explanation: Strict mode catches common coding mistakes: undeclared variables throw ReferenceError, duplicate params are errors, 'this' is undefined in functions (not window).

questions.question: What is a closure in JavaScript?

choices.A: A function that has been called and returned a value
choices.B: A function that retains access to variables from its outer (enclosing) scope even after the outer function has returned
choices.C: A function defined inside a class constructor
choices.D: A function that cannot be called more than once

questions.explanation: A closure is a function bundled with its lexical environment. It remembers variables from its outer scope even when executed outside that scope.

web_developer.source: IAAP WAS (Web Accessibility Specialist) / W3C WCAG 2.1 certification level

questions.question: According to WCAG 2.1 Level AA, what is the minimum contrast ratio for normal-sized text against its background?
questions.explanation: WCAG 2.1 SC 1.4.3 requires 4.5:1 for normal text and 3:1 for large text (18pt+ or 14pt bold) at Level AA.

questions.question: Which HTML element is the most semantically correct for a page's primary navigation?

choices.B: <nav role="navigation">
choices.C: <nav>

questions.explanation: <nav> is the semantic HTML5 element for navigation. Adding role='navigation' (B) is redundant -- the implicit role of <nav> is already 'navigation'.

questions.question: What CSS property creates a layout where child elements wrap to the next line when they exceed the container width?

choices.B: display: flex; flex-direction: column;
choices.C: display: block; overflow: hidden;

questions.explanation: flex-wrap: wrap allows flex items to wrap to the next line. flex-direction: column stacks vertically but doesn't wrap.

questions.question: An image is purely decorative (adds no information). What is the correct accessibility treatment?

choices.B: Remove the alt attribute entirely
choices.C: alt="" (empty alt attribute)

questions.explanation: Empty alt (alt='') tells screen readers to skip the image. Removing alt entirely causes screen readers to announce the filename. A non-empty alt would be announced.

questions.question: In CSS Grid, what does 'grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(250px, 1fr))' achieve?

choices.B: Creates responsive columns that are at least 250px wide, filling available space, wrapping as needed
choices.C: Creates a single column that is 250px wide
choices.D: Creates columns that are always exactly 1fr wide regardless of content

questions.explanation: auto-fit + minmax creates a responsive grid: columns are at least 250px, expand to fill space (1fr), and auto-wrap when the container is too narrow.

questions.question: Which ARIA role should be used for a modal dialog that requires user interaction before returning to the page?

choices.B: role="alertdialog"
choices.C: role="alert"

questions.explanation: alertdialog is for modal dialogs requiring user acknowledgment. dialog is for non-modal. alert is for dynamic notifications. tooltip is for hover info.

questions.question: What is the mobile-first approach to responsive design?

choices.A: Design for desktop first, then use max-width media queries to override for mobile
choices.B: Write base styles for mobile, then use min-width media queries to enhance for larger screens
choices.C: Create separate CSS files for mobile and desktop
choices.D: Use viewport units (vw, vh) for all sizing

questions.explanation: Mobile-first means base CSS targets small screens. min-width breakpoints progressively add complexity for wider viewports. This sends less CSS to mobile devices.

questions.question: A developer removes the default :focus outline on buttons with 'outline: none'. What accessibility problem does this create?

choices.A: Screen readers can no longer find the button
choices.B: Keyboard users cannot see which element currently has focus
choices.C: The button becomes unclickable on mobile devices
choices.D: The button loses its click event handler

questions.explanation: Removing focus outline makes it impossible for keyboard users to track their position on the page. WCAG 2.4.7 requires a visible focus indicator.

questions.question: What is the difference between 'rem' and 'em' units in CSS?

choices.A: rem is relative to the root element font-size; em is relative to the parent element font-size
choices.B: rem is for margins; em is for padding
choices.C: They are identical and interchangeable
choices.D: rem is a fixed unit; em is relative

questions.explanation: rem = root em (relative to <html> font-size, typically 16px). em = relative to the current element's or parent's font-size, which can compound.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four principles of WCAG (POUR)?

choices.B: Operable
choices.C: Universal

questions.explanation: POUR stands for Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust. 'Universal' is not one of the four principles.

security_agent.source: OWASP Top 10 2025 / CompTIA Security+ / CEH certification level

questions.question: Bob is logged in as a regular user. He discovers that by accessing https://example.com/admin/deleteUser?userId=500 he can delete user accounts, even though he's not an administrator. This is an example of:

choices.B: Vertical privilege escalation (elevation of privilege)
choices.C: Authentication failure

questions.explanation: A regular user performing admin actions is vertical privilege escalation. Horizontal would be accessing another user at the same privilege level.

questions.question: User Alice (ID=100) changes her profile URL from userId=100 to userId=200 and sees Bob's private data. This is an example of:

choices.B: Horizontal privilege escalation (IDOR)
choices.C: Client-side access control bypass

questions.explanation: Accessing another user's data at the same privilege level by manipulating a direct object reference is Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR), a form of horizontal privilege escalation.

questions.question: Which of the following is the MOST secure way to store user passwords?

choices.B: AES-256 encryption of the password
choices.C: bcrypt or Argon2 with a unique salt per user

questions.explanation: Adaptive hashing (bcrypt, Argon2, PBKDF2) with per-user salts is the standard. SHA-256 is too fast (brute-forceable). AES is reversible. Base64 is encoding, not security.

questions.question: Which OWASP Top 10 category does SQL injection fall under?

choices.B: A02:2021 Cryptographic Failures
choices.C: A03:2021 Injection

questions.explanation: SQL injection, XSS, command injection, and LDAP injection all fall under A03:2021 Injection in the OWASP Top 10.

questions.question: A developer implements access control on a user profile endpoint. What critical check is most commonly MISSING?

choices.B: Authorization check (should the current user access THIS specific resource?)
choices.C: Input validation (is the ID a valid format?)

questions.explanation: Many apps check authentication (who are you?) but skip authorization (are you allowed to access THIS resource?). This leads to IDOR and privilege escalation.

questions.question: What is the primary defense against Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)?

choices.A: Input validation on all form fields
choices.B: Using HTTPS for all requests
choices.C: Anti-CSRF tokens (synchronizer token pattern) or SameSite cookies

questions.explanation: CSRF tokens ensure the request came from your site's form. SameSite=Strict/Lax cookies prevent cross-origin cookie sending. HTTPS doesn't prevent CSRF.

questions.question: Is a parameterized SQL query completely safe from ALL injection attacks?

choices.A: Yes, completely safe from all injection
choices.B: No, parameterized queries are no better than string concatenation
choices.C: Yes for SQL injection, but other injection risks (OS command, LDAP) may still exist
choices.D: No, you must use an ORM instead

questions.explanation: Parameterized queries prevent SQL injection specifically. But the same input might be used in OS commands, LDAP queries, or other injection contexts that need separate defense.

questions.question: Which teams should include functional access control testing in unit and integration tests?

choices.B: Only QA engineers
choices.C: Developers and QA staff

questions.explanation: Access control testing should be embedded in development and QA processes, not siloed to security-only activities. Shift left.

questions.question: What is the primary risk of using eval() or exec() in Python with user-supplied input?

choices.A: Performance degradation due to interpretation overhead
choices.B: Arbitrary code execution -- the user can run any Python code on the server
choices.C: Memory leaks from uncollectable objects

questions.explanation: eval()/exec() with user input allows Remote Code Execution (RCE). An attacker could run os.system('rm -rf /') or exfiltrate data.

questions.question: A requirements.txt pins 'requests==2.28.0' which has a known CVE. What is the BEST remediation approach?

choices.A: Remove the pin and use latest
choices.B: Run pip-audit, review the CVE severity, and upgrade to the nearest patched version
choices.C: Switch to a different HTTP library
choices.D: Add a comment noting the vulnerability exists

questions.explanation: Audit first (understand the CVE), then upgrade to the nearest patched version. Unpinning (A) risks breaking changes. Switching libraries (C) is disproportionate.

questions.question: Which of the following is a good testing practice that applies regardless of the software development lifecycle being used?

choices.B: Step-by-step test case documentation
choices.C: Ensuring there is a corresponding testing activity for every software development activity
choices.D: Involving stakeholders in reviewing all acceptance criteria before designing test cases

questions.explanation: ISTQB principle: for every development activity, there should be a corresponding test activity. This applies to waterfall, agile, or any lifecycle.

questions.question: What is one potential benefit of using a test automation tool?

choices.A: The quality of defects will improve
choices.B: Ease of access to statistics about test progress
choices.C: Disk space is not required to track test artifacts

questions.explanation: Test automation tools typically provide dashboards and reports on test progress, pass rates, and coverage -- making statistics easily accessible.

questions.question: In an Agile development model, what defines 'done' for testing?

choices.B: Entry criteria
choices.C: Exit criteria

questions.explanation: Exit criteria (or Definition of Done in Agile) define when testing is complete -- e.g., all test cases passed, coverage met, no critical bugs open.

questions.question: What is the key difference between verification and validation in testing?

choices.A: Verification checks if we built the product right; validation checks if we built the right product
choices.B: Verification is done by developers; validation is done by testers
choices.C: Verification uses dynamic testing; validation uses static testing
choices.D: They are the same thing with different names

questions.explanation: Verification = 'Are we building it right?' (conformance to spec). Validation = 'Are we building the right thing?' (meets user needs).

questions.question: Which testing technique is MOST appropriate for testing a login form that accepts username (1-50 chars) and password (8-128 chars)?

choices.B: Boundary value analysis
choices.C: State transition testing

questions.explanation: Boundary value analysis tests at the edges of valid ranges: username at 0, 1, 50, 51 chars; password at 7, 8, 128, 129 chars. Most defects cluster at boundaries.

questions.question: A tester finds a bug but cannot consistently reproduce it. What should they do?

choices.A: Close the bug as 'not reproducible' and move on
choices.B: File the bug with all available information including environment, steps tried, and frequency observed
choices.C: Wait until they can reproduce it 100% of the time before filing
choices.D: Ask a developer to fix it verbally without filing a report

questions.explanation: Intermittent bugs are still bugs. File with all context (env, timing, frequency, logs). Developers may recognize the pattern. Closing without reporting loses information.

questions.question: What is regression testing?

choices.A: Testing new features before they are fully implemented
choices.B: Testing to confirm that recent code changes have not adversely affected existing functionality
choices.C: Testing the system under extreme load conditions
choices.D: Testing performed by end users in production

questions.explanation: Regression testing verifies that changes (bug fixes, new features, refactoring) haven't broken existing functionality. It's a key candidate for automation.

questions.question: Which severity classification is appropriate for: 'The company logo on the About page is 2px off-center'?

choices.B: High -- visual defect visible to all users
choices.C: Low/Cosmetic -- minor visual imperfection with no functional impact
choices.D: Medium -- should be fixed in the next sprint

questions.explanation: A 2px misalignment has zero functional impact and minimal user notice. Low/Cosmetic severity. Should not block a release.

questions.question: Which is a benefit of early stakeholder feedback during testing?

choices.A: It can be gathered early and used after the product goes to production
choices.B: The project manager gets a better view of changes for the next version
choices.C: The developers get a better understanding of what they are building
choices.D: The testers get better test automation requirements

questions.explanation: Early stakeholder feedback helps developers understand requirements and expectations, reducing misunderstandings and rework later.

questions.question: What is the difference between black-box and white-box testing?

choices.A: Black-box tests internal code structure; white-box tests external behavior
choices.B: Black-box tests external behavior without knowledge of internal code; white-box tests with knowledge of internal code structure
choices.C: Black-box is manual testing; white-box is automated testing
choices.D: Black-box is negative testing; white-box is positive testing

questions.explanation: Black-box = test inputs/outputs without seeing code (specification-based). White-box = test with knowledge of code structure (structural testing).

setup_guide.source: Ollama documentation / Hardware fundamentals / User onboarding best practices

questions.question: A user's hardware detection shows: gpu_name='NVIDIA RTX 3060', vram_mb=12288. What model would you recommend?

choices.A: llama3.1:70b -- the biggest model for best quality
choices.B: llama3.1:8b -- fits comfortably with room to spare
choices.C: phi3:mini -- always use the smallest model to be safe
choices.D: No model will work -- 12GB is insufficient for local AI

questions.explanation: 12GB VRAM comfortably runs 8B parameter models. 70B needs 40GB+ VRAM. phi3:mini would work but wastes the available VRAM. 12GB is excellent for local AI.

questions.question: After a user installs Ollama, what command should they run to verify the installation succeeded?

choices.B: ollama list
choices.C: ollama status

questions.explanation: 'ollama list' verifies both that the binary is on PATH and the service is running. It should return an empty list or installed models. A failed install would error.

questions.question: A user reports 'Error: model requires more system memory (8.0 GiB) than is available (6.0 GiB)'. What do you recommend?

choices.B: Try a smaller model like phi3:mini that fits in their available memory
choices.C: Delete other programs to free memory

questions.explanation: The error means the model is too large for available VRAM/RAM. The right fix is choosing a model that fits. Smaller models (phi3:mini, gemma:2b) work in 4-6GB.

questions.question: What does 'VRAM' stand for and why does it matter for running local AI models?

choices.A: Virtual Random Access Memory -- it determines download speed
choices.B: Video Random Access Memory -- it determines how large a model the GPU can run simultaneously
choices.C: Variable RAM -- it fluctuates based on system load
choices.D: Volatile Read-Access Memory -- it stores temporary GPU calculations

questions.explanation: VRAM is the GPU's dedicated memory. Model parameters must fit in VRAM for GPU-accelerated inference. More VRAM = larger models = better quality responses.

questions.question: A user's hardware detection shows cpu_only=True. How should you frame this to the user?

choices.A: 'Your computer cannot run AI models. You need a GPU.'
choices.B: 'Your computer can still run AI models, just a bit slower. Smaller models like phi3:mini work great on CPU.'
choices.C: 'You should buy a new computer with a GPU.'
choices.D: 'CPU mode is actually faster than GPU for most tasks.'

questions.explanation: CPU-only is slower but functional. The setup guide should be encouraging, set realistic expectations, and recommend appropriately-sized models.

questions.question: What is Ollama?

choices.A: A cloud-based AI service that requires a subscription
choices.B: A tool for running large language models locally on your own computer
choices.C: A programming language for building AI applications
choices.D: A GPU driver required for AI acceleration

questions.explanation: Ollama is a free, open-source tool that downloads and runs LLMs locally. No cloud, no subscription, data stays on the user's machine.

questions.question: Which command pulls a model in Ollama?

choices.B: ollama download llama3.1:8b
choices.C: ollama pull llama3.1:8b

questions.explanation: 'ollama pull' downloads a model from the Ollama registry. It uses Docker-like terminology (pull/push).

questions.question: A user asks 'Is my data sent to the cloud when I use Cohort with Ollama?' What is the correct answer?

choices.A: Yes, all data is processed in the cloud
choices.B: No, everything runs locally on your computer. Your data never leaves your machine.
choices.C: Only metadata is sent to improve the service
choices.D: It depends on your internet connection

questions.explanation: Ollama runs models entirely locally. No data is sent to any cloud service. This is a key selling point for privacy-conscious users.

questions.question: When introducing Cohort's agents to a new user, what is the best approach?

choices.A: List all agents with full capability descriptions in one message
choices.B: Introduce agents one at a time with a single sentence each, letting the user ask follow-up questions
choices.C: Skip the introduction and let users discover agents on their own
choices.D: Only mention the most advanced agent to impress the user

questions.explanation: Avoid information overload. Introduce progressively: one sentence per agent, let curiosity drive deeper exploration. New users absorb better in small chunks.

questions.question: A setup guide recommends running 'curl -fsSL https://ollama.com/install.sh | sh' on Linux. What security caveat should accompany this?

choices.A: No caveat needed -- it's a standard install method
choices.B: Always verify the URL and source before piping to shell. This is the official install script from ollama.com.
choices.C: Never use curl for installation -- always download manually
choices.D: Run the command with sudo for extra security

questions.explanation: Pipe-to-shell is convenient but users should verify the URL is legitimate. A good setup guide acknowledges this practice while providing the official source.
